name: Belgium
summary: Belgium offers Dhaka-decided study visas, abundant English CS master's and a clear study->search-year->single-permit->5-year-PR route with dual citizenship allowed; but Europe's highest tax wedge (52.5%), a 2025 citizenship reform raising language to B1 plus a civics exam and a €1,000 fee, the De Wever 'strictest migration ever' climate, and a study-phase spouse work restriction temper it for two engineers.

links.title: Immigration Office (IBZ/DOFI, official)
Visa for Belgium if you live in Bangladesh (FPS Foreign Affairs, official)

job-market.summary: Persistent IT shortage; ~€42-78k SW pay in Brussels/Antwerp, English-friendly tech scene absorbs both engineers.
job-market.reasoning: Belgium has a structural ICT-profile shortage (software developers, analysts and data roles are on regional shortage-occupation lists in Flanders, Brussels and Wallonia), and 2026 levels.fyi/PayScale data put SW engineer pay at ~€42,000-78,451 gross with Brussels/Antwerp totals ~€54,000-58,000. Brussels' international and EU-institution scene plus Flanders' tech hubs operate substantially in English, so two CS graduates can realistically both be placed. Slightly below top-tier because the mid-market is narrow and sponsorship requires meeting region-specific salary floors.
evidence: ICT roles on shortage-occupation lists in all three regions (VDAB/Actiris/Forem)
SW engineer pay ~€42,000-78,451 gross (levels.fyi/PayScale 2026)
Brussels avg total comp ~€54,270; Antwerp ~€57,831 (levels.fyi 2026)
Brussels EU/international scene + Flanders hubs work in English
AI/specialist stacks drive most 2026 rate growth

links.title: Highly qualified worker - Belgium (EU Immigration Portal, official)
Software Engineer Salary in Belgium (levels.fyi)

visa-access.summary: D study visa filed and decided from Dhaka VAC (opened 16 Nov 2025); no New Delhi travel; €180 fee, ~15-45 days.
visa-access.reasoning: FPS Foreign Affairs confirms applicants resident in Bangladesh apply at the Belgian Visa Application Centre in Dhaka (Banani) with appointments live since 16 November 2025 and explicitly do NOT need to travel to New Delhi. The long-stay D visa costs €180 plus a 12,325 BDT VAC service fee, with the Immigration Office making the final decision and processing typically 15 days up to 45 calendar days. Friction remains around peak-season appointment backlogs (May-Sep student intakes) and the financial-proof requirement, but local decisioning makes this strong for a Bangladeshi applicant.
evidence: Belgian VAC in Dhaka (Banani); appointments since 16 Nov 2025
FPS Foreign Affairs: applicants do NOT travel to New Delhi
D long-stay visa €180 + 12,325 BDT VAC service fee
Processing typically 15 days, up to 45 calendar days
Final decision by the Immigration Office (DOFI)

VFS Global Belgium - Bangladesh (official partner)

citizenship.summary: 5-year integration route + dual citizenship allowed, but 2025 reform raised language to B1, added civics exam and €1,000 fee.
citizenship.reasoning: Belgium permits naturalisation after 5 years of legal residence (with integration/economic participation) or 10 years without, and dual citizenship has been allowed since 2008, so Bangladeshis need not renounce. However, the Program Law of 18 July 2025 raised the application fee from €150 to €1,000 (annually indexed from 2026) and the 2025 reform lifted the language bar from A2 to at least B1 in one official language plus a new civics exam on institutions and values. The 5yr+dual basics sit near the strong band, but the tightened language and cost pull it down.
evidence: Naturalisation at 5 years (integration) or 10 years (no integration)
Dual citizenship allowed since 2008; no renunciation for Bangladeshis
2025 reform: language raised from A2 to B1 + new civics exam
Application fee €1,000 from 18 Jul 2025 (was €150), indexed from 2026
Mandatory integration: language, employment, financial independence

Belgian Citizenship 2025/2026 guide (Immigrant in Law)

post-study-work.summary: 12-month 'search year' to find work or start a business after graduation, then switch to single permit in-country.
post-study-work.reasoning: Non-EU graduates of a Belgian higher-education institution (level 5+) can request a 12-month 'search year' (orientation year) residence permit to find qualified work or set up a business, needing ~€835/month resources for 2025-2026. During it they can take any job and, once an employer applies, switch in-country to a combined single permit. Both partners as graduates can each hold their own search-year permit. It falls short of the 3-year open-work top anchor because the window is only 12 months.
evidence: 12-month search/orientation year for level-5+ graduates
Resources ~€835/month required (2025-2026)
Any employment allowed during the search year
In-country switch to combined single permit once hired
Both graduates can each hold their own search-year permit

links.title: Work in Flanders after graduation (Study in Flanders, official)
Orientation year (University of Antwerp)

spouse-family.summary: Both work freely in work/Blue Card phase, but a spouse joining a STUDENT has no automatic work right - a study-phase gap.
spouse-family.reasoning: Spouses of single-permit/EU Blue Card workers may work, and for highly-qualified workers and Blue Card holders the family-reunification income threshold is presumed met, so the work phase is fine for this couple. The decisive weakness is the study phase: a spouse reuniting with a student or PhD holder has NO automatic right to work and needs a separate employer-sponsored single permit. Because both are engineers, the practical fix is for each to enrol as a student (each then works ~20h/week as a student), so the penalty is partial rather than a hard dependant ban.
evidence: Spouse of single-permit/Blue Card worker may work (DOFI)
No automatic work right for spouse reuniting with a student/PhD
Family-reunification net-income threshold €2,323.08/month (Jul 2025)
Threshold presumed met for Blue Card / highly-qualified workers
Workaround: both enrol as students, each works ~20h/week

links.title: Family reunification - holder of a single permit (DOFI, official)
Bringing your family (University of Antwerp)

msc-study.summary: Many English CS master's (KU Leuven, Ghent, VUB); non-EU tuition ~€1,800-8,000/yr; ~20h/wk work; spouse can accompany.
msc-study.reasoning: Flemish and Brussels universities offer abundant English-taught CS/engineering master's (KU Leuven, Ghent, VUB, Antwerp), with non-EU tuition roughly €1,800-8,000/year (KU Leuven averaging ~€4,100) - far cheaper than Anglophone destinations though not free. French-speaking universities raised non-EU fees to ~€5,010/year for 2025-2026. Non-EEA students may work up to 20h/week (650-hour annual student quota in 2025) and a spouse can accompany via family reunification. Scholarships (Master Mind, VLIR-UOS) exist but are competitive.
evidence: English CS master's at KU Leuven, Ghent, VUB, Antwerp
Non-EU tuition ~€1,800-8,000/yr; KU Leuven avg ~€4,100
French-speaking universities non-EU fee ~€5,010 (2025-2026)
Non-EEA students work up to 20h/week; 650h annual quota (2025)
Flanders Master Mind / VLIR-UOS scholarships available

Tuition fee regulations 2025-2026 (KU Leuven)

pr-pathway.summary: EU long-term resident / permanent residence after 5 years' continuous legal stay; study time counts at 50%.
pr-pathway.reasoning: Third-country nationals can obtain EU long-term resident status / permanent residence (B, K or L card) after 5 years of continuous legal residence, provided they show stable sufficient resources and health insurance - readily met by two engineers. Crucially DOFI counts half of study periods toward the 5-year total, shortening the effective work-phase wait, with a ~5-month decision window. It sits at the standard-5yr anchor: clear and rule-based but not fast, and the De Wever government's 5-year benefit exclusion for newcomers raises the self-sufficiency bar.
evidence: EU long-term resident status after 5 years' continuous residence
Half of study periods count toward the 5-year total (DOFI)
Requires stable sufficient resources + health insurance
~5-month decision after acknowledgement of receipt
Newcomers barred from social benefits for first 5 years (2025 govt)

income-cost.summary: Decent gross SW pay but Europe's highest tax wedge (52.5%) and ~€1,100-1,320 Brussels rents erode two-income savings.
income-cost.reasoning: Gross SW pay (~€54,000-58,000 total in Brussels/Antwerp) looks reasonable, but Belgium has the OECD's highest tax wedge at 52.5% (2025) and take-home for an average single worker is only 60.5% of gross, so net income is heavily compressed. Brussels one-bed rents average ~€1,110-1,321 in 2025-2026; living costs ~€1,760/month for one. Two engineering incomes still permit savings, but high deductions and rising rents keep this in the mixed band versus lower-tax peers.
evidence: OECD highest tax wedge 52.5% for average single worker (2025)
Take-home only 60.5% of gross vs OECD avg 74.9%
Brussels 1-bed rent ~€1,110-1,321/month (2025-2026)
Single-person living cost ~€1,760/month
Two incomes still save, but net is heavily eroded

healthcare.summary: Universal mutuelle/ziekenfonds, high quality, free choice of doctor; ~10-20% patient co-pays remain.
healthcare.reasoning: Belgium runs a universal compulsory health-insurance system via a mutuelle/ziekenfonds funded by social contributions (~4.7% employee withholding in 2025) plus a ~€64 annual fund fee, with high quality - 86% of residents satisfied with availability of quality care vs an OECD average of 64% - and free choice of GP/specialist without referral. Slightly below the universal-and-free top anchor because patients still pay ~10-20% co-pays on care and 15-50% on most prescriptions, though reimbursement and ceilings limit exposure.
evidence: Universal mutuelle/ziekenfonds; joining mandatory for residents
86% satisfied with quality care vs OECD avg 64%
Free choice of GP/specialist without referral
Patient co-pays ~10-20% on care; 15-50% on prescriptions
~€64 annual fund fee (2025); ~4.7% employee contribution

culture-language.summary: English widely usable in Brussels/Flanders tech, but Dutch/French needed for officialdom and B1 for citizenship.
culture-language.reasoning: English works well day-to-day in Brussels' international scene and Flemish tech workplaces, and high livability and English-taught study lower the entry barrier. But Belgium is officially trilingual (Dutch/French/German), so an official language is needed for bureaucracy, integration and especially naturalisation, where the 2025 reform now demands B1 plus a civics exam. The language-region split (Dutch in Flanders, French in Wallonia/Brussels) adds friction, holding this in the mixed band given the couple's citizenship goal.
evidence: English widely usable in Brussels + Flemish tech workplaces
Dutch/French required for officialdom and integration
Citizenship now needs B1 + civics exam (2025 reform)
Trilingual country; Flanders Dutch, Wallonia/Brussels French
High livability and English-taught study ease initial entry

safety-law.summary: WJP 2025: ~17th of 143 (score ~0.78); strong rule of law and fair courts, moderate urban crime in Brussels.
safety-law.reasoning: Belgium sits in the upper tier of the WJP Rule of Law Index 2025 (around 17th of 143, score ~0.78), with strong constraints on government, independent courts and an established anti-discrimination framework. Crime is generally low though Brussels has elevated property crime and occasional drug-gang violence. Solid legal protections for residents and migrants, a notch below the Nordic top tier (Denmark 1, Norway 2) that dominates this index.
evidence: WJP Rule of Law Index 2025: ~17th of 143, score ~0.78
Top tier dominated by Denmark (1), Norway (2), Finland (3)
Independent judiciary; established anti-discrimination law
Generally low crime; elevated Brussels property/drug crime
Fair legal protections for residents and migrants

politics.summary: De Wever 'Arizona' coalition (Jan 2025) launched 'strictest migration ever', but targets work/study migration positively.
politics.reasoning: A five-party 'Arizona' coalition under N-VA's Bart de Wever took office 31 January 2025 promising the 'strictest migration policy ever' - tougher family reunification, scaled-back subsidiary protection and a 5-year social-benefit exclusion for newcomers. Institutions are stable and the government explicitly says it wants to attract work and study migrants who contribute, which protects skilled routes for this couple. The overall anti-immigration tightening and a strong Flemish-nationalist tone hold politics in the mixed band.
evidence: De Wever 'Arizona' coalition formed 31 Jan 2025 (N-VA-led)
Government brands its policy 'strictest migration ever'
Stricter family reunification; subsidiary protection scaled back
Newcomers barred from social benefits for first 5 years
Stated focus on attracting work/study migrants who contribute

tax.summary: Europe's heaviest labour tax: OECD tax wedge 52.5% (2025), take-home only 60.5% of gross for a single worker.
tax.reasoning: Belgium had the highest tax wedge among 38 OECD countries in 2025 at 52.5% for the average single worker, leaving take-home at just 60.5% of gross versus an OECD average of 74.9%. Income tax and employer social contributions make up 79% of the wedge, and progressive income-tax rates reach 50%. A married couple with two children fares better (6th-highest wedge, 36.9%), but for two childless high earners the burden is severe, placing this near the bottom band.
evidence: OECD highest tax wedge: 52.5% single worker (2025)
Take-home only 60.5% of gross vs OECD avg 74.9%
Top marginal income-tax rate 50%
Income tax + employer SSC = 79% of the wedge
Married-with-2-children wedge 36.9% (6th highest)

muslim-diaspora.summary: ~6% Muslim (~700k), 300+ mosques and easy halal, but the Bangladeshi community is tiny (~5k) and sentiment is tense.
muslim-diaspora.reasoning: Islam is Belgium's second religion at ~6% of the population (~600,000-700,000), with 300+ mosques (some sources 350+) and halal food readily available in restaurants and supermarkets, so religious infrastructure is good in cities. However, the specifically Bangladeshi community is very small (~5,000), so South-Asian familiarity is limited, and NGOs (CIIB, Amnesty) report ongoing Islamophobic discrimination and harassment amid the De Wever government's hardline migration tone. Decent halal/worship access but a thin BD network and tense sentiment keep this mid-band.
evidence: Muslims ~6% of population (~600,000-700,000)
300+ mosques (some sources 350+); halal widely available
Bangladeshi community very small (~5,000)
CIIB/Amnesty report Islamophobic discrimination and harassment
